visualization: The S-layer is depicted as a continuous, paracrystalline proteinaceous layer coating the entire outer surface of the archaeal cell, shown in Figure 1a and 5a with near-perfect hexagonal continuity and pentameric defects at edges.

The NmSLP monomer is visualized as a 'cashew'-shaped structure composed of ten immunoglobulin-like domains, with the first eight resolved at high resolution (3.3–4.5 Å) in cryo-ET maps (Fig. 1e–g), arranged into a hexameric unit.

The hexameric array is shown as a pseudohexagonal lattice formed by six NmSLP monomers, with C6 symmetry broken at the N terminus to form a C2-symmetric central pore; this arrangement is mapped across the cell surface in Figure 1b and 5a.

Glycan decoration is visualized as 17 distinct densities per NmSLP monomer projecting outward from asparagine residues, forming a thick sugar-rich coat around the cell surface, as seen in the cryo-EM map (Fig. 2k).

The cell membrane is represented as a lipid bilayer beneath the S-layer, where ammonium ions are ultimately oxidized; it is exposed only when the S-layer is disrupted by EGTA treatment, as shown in Figure 3f.

terms: Molecular dynamics simulation of ion binding
visualization: Ammonium binding and channelling is visualized as a multi-step process: ammonium ions bind to negatively charged pores in the S-layer (Fig. 2b–j), move along a charge gradient toward the cell-facing side (Fig. 4c), and diffuse to the membrane for oxidation (Fig. 5c); this is supported by ITC heat release curves (Fig. 3a,b) and MD simulations showing ammonium accumulation near the membrane.

S-layer disassembly by EGTA is visualized as a progressive structural disruption: 2.5 mM EGTA causes gaps in the S-layer (Fig. 3e), while 5 mM EGTA leads to complete loss of the S-layer and exposure of the naked membrane (Fig. 3f), correlating with abolished ammonium binding (Fig. 3a,b).

Cryo-ET imaging workflow is visualized through tomographic slices showing cellular ultrastructure (Fig. 1a), subtomogram averaging mapping hexamer/pentamer positions (Fig. 1b,c), and high-resolution derivation of atomic models directly from whole cells (Fig. 1d–g).

Single-particle cryo-EM structure determination is visualized as a process starting with purified S-layer sheets (Fig. 2a), followed by particle picking, 2D classification, 3D reconstruction yielding a 2.7-Å map, and model building revealing detailed pore architecture and glycan locations (Fig. 2k).

Molecular dynamics simulation of ion binding is visualized as residue-based ammonium occupancy plotted onto the S-layer structure (Fig. 4a), with high-occupancy acidic residues matching pore locations (Fig. 4b), and histograms showing ammonium accumulation near the membrane-proximal side (Fig. 4c).

visualization: The DNA minicircle is depicted as a closed, circular double helix that is constrained into a figure-eight shape (plectonemic superhelix). The overall structure is shown in grey, with specific regions highlighted in color.

Site 1 is labeled in red and located at the bottom of the plectoneme loop. It is visually represented as a region where the DNA backbone is sharply bent, indicated by a red curved segment and an inset showing a local disruption with a red dot representing a flipped-out base.

Site 2 is labeled in blue and located at the top of the plectoneme loop, diametrically opposite to Site 1. It is shown as a blue curved segment, with an inset depicting a similar local structural disruption with a red dot.

Site 3 is labeled in green and positioned on the side of the DNA minicircle, not at a superhelical apex. It is shown as a green curved segment, indicating it is part of the main body of the loop rather than at a sharp bend.

The Superhelical apex is the point of maximum curvature in the plectonemic structure, visually represented by the tight bends at the top and bottom of the figure-eight, where Sites 2 and 1 are localized, respectively.
The Plectoneme loop is the interwound, figure-eight conformation of the DNA minicircle. It is composed of two lobes connected at the center, with Sites 1 and 2 situated at the apices of these lobes.

Base pair disruption is visualized as a small red dot within an inset magnified view of the DNA double helix at Sites 1 and 2. This represents a single base flipping out of the helix, causing a local structural perturbation.

Mechanical correlation is depicted by a red dashed line with an arrow pointing from Site 2 to Site 1. This line connects the two sites across the DNA minicircle, illustrating the transfer of mechanical stress or strain from one site to the other.

Writhe facilitation is shown by the transformation of the DNA structure from a less compact form to a more tightly writhed figure-eight. The process is indicated by a large black arrow pointing right. The text explains that base pair disruption at Site 1 allows the plectoneme loop to become smaller, which facilitates writhe.

Cooperative denaturation is illustrated by the reciprocal relationship between Sites 1 and 2. The diagram shows that disruption at Site 2 leads to bending at Site 1, which in turn causes further disruption at Site 2, creating a feedback loop. This is supported by the bidirectional nature of the mechanical correlation and the depiction of both sites undergoing disruption.
